informal: Suppose $E\subset\mathbb{R}^k$ is uncountable, and let $P$ be the set of condensation points of $E$. Prove that at most countably many points of $E$ are not in $P$.
lean:
theorem Rudin_exercise_2_27b (k : ℕ) (E P : Set (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin k)))
  (hE : ¬ Set.Countable E)
  (hP : P = {x | ∀ U ∈ 𝓝 x, ¬ Set.Countable (U ∩ E)}) :
  Set.Countable (E \ P)
reasoning: The informal statement says E ⊂ ℝ^k is uncountable, P is the set of condensation points of E, and asks to show at most countably many points of E are not in P, i.e., E \ P is countable. The Lean signature uses EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin k) which is the standard formalization of ℝ^k, assumes E is uncountable (¬ Countable E), defines P as the set of points x such that every neighborhood U of x has uncountable intersection with E — this is the standard definition of condensation point of E. The conclusion Set.Countable (E \ P) matches "at most countably many points of E are not in P." The encoding is faithful.
